EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance moved across the Billings Forecast Area during the late morning hours on the 13th that resulted in severe thunderstorms that lasted into the early morning hours of the 14th. Strong daytime heating along with 40-50 knots of southwesterly mid-level flow resulted in a very unstable atmosphere that was conducive for supercell thunderstorms.

Strong to severe thunderstorms erupted over the eastern Wind River Mountains during the morning hours and moved east-northeast across Fremont and Natrona counties. A favorable wind profile helped the storms become severe and produce hail up to the size of baseballs at Powder River. There were numerous reports of quarter to golf ball sized hail in a swath from Castle Gardens to around Natrona. As the storm approached Casper at least three funnel clouds were observed, one of which briefly touched down northeast of Evansville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicles were forced to pull over near Powder River as baseball sized hail fell from a second severe thunderstorm.

Lightning ignited the Fairfield Hill Wildfire which roared to life on Monday morning, July 22. The fire eventually scorched 1,335 acres on the hills above Lander and was seen from many miles. The fire threatened a community of homes and several recreational sites in Sinks Canyon forcing evacuations in those areas within the first hours of the fire. The Sinks Canyon Road was closed as the fire worked its way to the bottom of the canyon near the road. The fire was most active the first day and night as aggressive air and ground support was called in to assist firefighting efforts. No structures were lost to the fire which cost over $3 million to fight. Full containment of the fire came on July 29.
An unstable atmosphere allowed for some thunderstorms to develop on the afternoon of July 3. One storm nearly became severe over southern Johnson County with reports of hail up to nickel size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A near severe thunderstorm moved south-southeast across southern Johnson County. Ranchers reported hail up to the size of nickels. Heavy rain caused minor flooding at ranches around Mayoworth. Several hay and barley fields were damaged due to the combination of hail and rain. Other damage included broken camper windows and rear-view mirrors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist air mass, upper level disturbance and weak flow aloft combined to produce slow-moving thunderstorms. One thunderstorm dropped an estimated 2 to 3 inches of rain across southwest Johnson County and caused flash flooding in and around Barnum.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell in and around Barnum producing minor flash flooding. Some mud and debris was deposited by the flood on Barnum Road, which was removed by a county grading crew. Beaver Creek overflowed its banks and flooded a few ranch fields. The inundation of water caused a five foot rise in about two hours on the Middle Fork of the Powder River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass and an upper level disturbance brought a third straight day of thunderstorms to western and central Wyoming. The strongest thunderstorm formed over the Absaroka Range and become severe as it moved east across the Big Horn Basin and into Johnson County. There were numerous reports of large hail in a swath from near Barnum to Midwest with the largest stones being ping pong ball sized in Midwest. The storm also brought wind gusts up to 60 mph. In addition, a wind gust from a collapsing thunderstorm in Sublette County produced a 59 mph wind gust at the Big Piney Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hot Springs County emergency manager reported a 60 mph wind gust at Kirby.

There were numerous reports of large hail in a swath from near Barnum to Midwest with the largest stones being ping pong ball sized in Midwest. The storm also brought wind gusts up to 60 mph. In addition, a wind gust from a collapsing thunderstorm in Sublette County produced a 59 mph wind gust at the Big Piney Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five large cottonwood trees were toppled and limbs up to one foot in diameter were ripped to the ground by wind gusts estimated up to 80 mph. At a ranch along West Willow Creek Road, one large feeding shed was gradually torn apart by the wind while another was rolled.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall estimated by radar to be 1 to 1.5 inches in less than 30 minutes inundated areas south of Barnum and into northern Natrona County. Blue Creek was flooded as were Poker Creek and some smaller streams feeding the Middle Fork of the Powder River. Several corrals were lost and damage was done to at least one out-building. One observer said it was the worst flooding they had seen in 27 years at their residence. Bar C Gap ran 6 to 7 feet deep and Bar C Ranch Road was covered with mud and debris.||Another hard hit area centered on Willow Creek and nearby drainages along the TTT-Lone Bear Road and West Willow Creek Road. Water was running over the roads in more than one location.

Heavy rain of one-half to one inch fell in about 25 minutes across the east side of Casper and portions of the Sheep Herder Hill Burn Scar. The result was street flooding along and near East 2nd Street in Casper and more significant flooding along the Clear Fork of Muddy Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain began falling around 1900MST over east Casper and the east end of Casper Mountain. Thunderstorms had approached the area from the southwest and a low-level northeast flow made for a slower progression over east Casper. The result was rainfall of one-half to one-inch in about 25 minutes. Street flooding was observed along East 2nd Street from Wyoming Boulevard toward Hat Six Road. The water was up to two feet deep near the intersection of Blackmore Road and East 2nd Street. Hail around one-half inch in diameter accumulated to a depth of several inches.||The most significant flooding occurred at the Hat Six Ranch at the east end of Casper Mountain. The steep canyons and hillsides above the ranch were torched the previous September by the Sheep Herder Hill Wildfire. Excessive rainfall quickly brought a mix of rock, mud, and debris down one canyon and another draw damaging two homes, at least three vehicles, and two all-terrain vehicles. Water and debris eventually found the Clear Fork Muddy Creek channel and spread out to be anywhere from 50 to 100 yards wide. One additional ranch received damage as the creek swept northeast and eventually topped the Hat Six Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved out of the Great Basin and into northern Colorado and southern Wyoming February 8 through 10. Snow spread across much of southern and central Wyoming on the afternoon and evening of February 8 and continued in some areas until the morning of February 10. Snowfall rates of over 1 inch an hour were reported in association with some of the more intense snow. Areas to receive the heaviest snow were around Lander and on Casper Mountain. Snowfall topping one foot was commonplace around Casper with up to 21 inches on the nearby hillsides. Casper Mountain observed 16 inches with over 20 inches estimated at two SNOTEL stations. Southern portions of the Star Valley saw 8 to 15 inches of snow. Many other locations around Fremont County and on the south side of Casper reported over 6 inches of snow. The maximum across Sweetwater County was 9 inches of snow at Wamsutter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured 16 inches of snow on Casper Mountain. Estimated snowfall at two SNOTEL stations topped 20 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved out of the Great Basin and into northern Colorado and southern Wyoming February 8 through 10. Snow spread across much of southern and central Wyoming on the afternoon and evening of February 8 and continued in some areas until the morning of February 10. Snowfall rates of over 1 inch an hour were reported in association with some of the more intense snow. Areas to receive the heaviest snow were around Lander and on Casper Mountain. Snowfall topping one foot was commonplace around Casper with up to 21 inches on the nearby hillsides. Casper Mountain observed 16 inches with over 20 inches estimated at two SNOTEL stations. Southern portions of the Star Valley saw 8 to 15 inches of snow. Many other locations around Fremont County and on the south side of Casper reported over 6 inches of snow. The maximum across Sweetwater County was 9 inches of snow at Wamsutter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured 8 inches of snow at Wamsutter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm system moved across eastern Colorado into western Kansas. Strong northerly winds gusting as high as 55 mph combined with falling snow to create periods of visibilities less than a quarter mile during the morning and afternoon across portions of southeast Wyoming.  Moderate to heavy snow fell over the Snowy and Sierra Madre ranges, with estimates of six to thirteen inches.  Snow amounts from the Laramie Valley to Cheyenne measured one to four inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sage Creek Basin SNOTEL site (elevation 7850 ft) estimated 13.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm moved across the Central Plains, bringing snow and gusty winds to much of northeastern Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell across the Wyoming Black Hills, where nine inches of snow was reported. Wind gusts to 40 mph, combined with the snow, reduced visibilities to around a quarter of a mile at times.

A potent winter storm system moved across eastern Colorado into western Kansas. Strong northerly winds gusting as high as 55 mph combined with falling snow to create periods of visibilities less than a quarter mile during the morning and afternoon across portions of southeast Wyoming.  Moderate to heavy snow fell over the Snowy and Sierra Madre ranges, with estimates of six to thirteen inches.  Snow amounts from the Laramie Valley to Cheyenne measured one to four inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several WYDOT sensors observed sustained winds of 35 to 45 mph with gusts to 55 mph.  Visibilities were reduced to a quarter mile in falling and blowing snow.  Two to three inches of snow was reported, mainly near the Interstate 80 Summit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system moved across southeast Wyoming.  Bitter cold air from the north combined with abundant moisture to produce widespread moderate to heavy snowfall.  Storm totals ranged from 6 to 18 inches, heaviest over the mountains.  Westerly winds of 30 to 40 mph produced blowing snow, poor visibilities and very cold wind chill temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to 12 inches of snow with one to two foot drifts was reported countywide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large pressure gradient combined with a very fast upper level jet produced a prolonged period of very strong and gusty gap winds through the wind corridors of southeast Wyoming. Winds frequently gusted between 60 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at Lone Tree measured sustained winds of 40 mph or higher, with a peak gust of 68 mph at 10/0211 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across the central Rockies and brought accumulating snow and gusty winds to the Snowy and Sierra Madre mountains.  Snow totals ranged from 6 to 12 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blackhall Mountain SNOTEL site (elevation 9820 ft) estimated 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm moved out of Utah and into southern Colorado setting the stage for heavy snow on Casper Mountain. There were numerous reports of snowfall ranging from 1 to 2 feet, including 22 inches at the Hogadon Ski Area and an estimated 23 inches at the Casper Mountain SNOTEL. Isolated reports of up to 3 feet were received from spotters on Casper Mountain and nearby Muddy Mountain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hogadon Ski Area on Casper Mountain reported 22 inches of new Snow. The Casper Mountain SNOTEL estimated 23 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved out of the Great Basin and into northern Colorado and southern Wyoming February 8 through 10. Snow spread across much of southern and central Wyoming on the afternoon and evening of February 8 and continued in some areas until the morning of February 10. Snowfall rates of over 1 inch an hour were reported in association with some of the more intense snow. Areas to receive the heaviest snow were around Lander and on Casper Mountain. Snowfall topping one foot was commonplace around Casper with up to 21 inches on the nearby hillsides. Casper Mountain observed 16 inches with over 20 inches estimated at two SNOTEL stations. Southern portions of the Star Valley saw 8 to 15 inches of snow. Many other locations around Fremont County and on the south side of Casper reported over 6 inches of snow. The maximum across Sweetwater County was 9 inches of snow at Wamsutter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported over 12 inches of snow at some areas in Sinks Canyon State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across western and central Wyoming and brought snow and high wind to the region. Snow amounts across the Cody Foothills generally ranged from 4 to 7 inches, with the most snow reported at Meeteetse. In addition, strong wind was reported with gusts to 68 mph in the Green and Rattlesnake Range on January 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported 2-4 inches of snow fell across Southern Johnson County. This combined with wind gusts to 40 mph sharply reduced visibility and brought very difficult travel conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sage Creek SNOTEL site (elevation 7850 ft) estimated 30 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche occurred on the southeast slope of Survey Peak around 1700MST and killed one skier. The skier was the last of a group to descend the slope when he triggered the slide that swept him 220 feet downhill and pinned him against a tree. The depth of the slide was estimated at 26 inches across a 230-foot crown. Friends ascended the slide path and found the deceased skier after about 15 minutes.

A slow moving Pacific low pressure system interacted with a stationary front and moist upslope flow over southeast Wyoming.  Significant snow accumulations occurred with 6 to 18 inches over lower elevations and two to three feet over the mountains.  Gusty north to northeast winds of 25 to 35 mph combined with the falling snow, which produced considerable blowing and drifting snow.  Numerous highways, including Interstates 25 and 80 were closed due to very low visibilities and icy, snow-packed surfaces.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer three miles east of Douglas measured 13.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous Pacific low pressure system brought widespread heavy snowfall and northerly winds up to 50 mph as it moved across the central Rockies.  Storm total snow accumulations up to two feet were observed across southeast Wyoming.  The combination of falling snow and strong winds produced considerable drifting snow, which resulted in very low visibilities and snow packed roads.  Numerous highways, including Interstate 80 from Rawlins to the Nebraska border were closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in Wheatland measured eight inches of snow.

Gusty north to northeast winds of 25 to 35 mph combined with the falling snow, which produced considerable blowing and drifting snow.  Numerous highways, including Interstates 25 and 80 were closed due to very low visibilities and icy, snow-packed surfaces.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer 21 miles north-northeast of Rolling Hills Estates measured 11 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same cold and slow-moving storm system that impacted southern Utah also brought heavy snowfall to extreme southwest Wyoming, in the form of wraparound precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mountain View, Wyoming received 9 inches of new snow for the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous and fast moving storm brought a period of heavy snow to the western mountains and high winds to many portions of western and central Wyoming. Up to 9 inches of snow fell in the higher elevations of Park and Teton Counties as well as portions of Yellowstone National Park. Lesser amounts fell in the Wind River Range where white-out conditions were reported over and around South Pass. In addition to the snow, high wind was reported across much of central and southwest Wyoming. Winds gusted over 70 mph in many areas, including 71 mph at Bitter Creek, 73 mph at Red Canyon, and 75 mph near Lander. The strong winds caused damage in Rock Springs, where several trees were downed, including one that crushed a police car. A telephone pole was also downed and 3 businesses lost their signs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Casper Airport ASOS reported a wind gust of 58 mph. At Hiland 40 mph sustained winds were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across Colorado and produced heavy snowfall over portions of southeast Wyoming. Storm total accumulations ranged from 6 to 20 inches, highest over the southern Laramie Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer six miles west of Buford measured an additional 13 inches of snow since midnight for a storm total of 20 inches.

Up to 9 inches of snow fell in the higher elevations of Park and Teton Counties as well as portions of Yellowstone National Park. Lesser amounts fell in the Wind River Range where white-out conditions were reported over and around South Pass. In addition to the snow, high wind was reported across much of central and southwest Wyoming. Winds gusted over 70 mph in many areas, including 71 mph at Bitter Creek, 73 mph at Red Canyon, and 75 mph near Lander. The strong winds caused damage in Rock Springs, where several trees were downed, including one that crushed a police car. A telephone pole was also downed and 3 businesses lost their signs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind gusts over 60 mph were reported across the Lander Foothills. Some gusts include 75 mph at the North Fork of the Popo Agie river, and 68 mph at the Lander Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts developed for a brief time across northern Campbell County after a cold front passed through the area. Wind gusts over 65 mph were reported near the Montana border.

A 40-year-old man was ascending a chute on the north face of Prospectors Mountain in Grand Teton National Park when an avalanche occurred above him. The slide, which triggered around 1030MST, carried the victim about 1,000 vertical feet from the Apocalypse Couloir.

Strong winds aloft mixed down to the surface and brought high winds to portions of eastern Sweetwater County. Winds were sustained over 40 mph at several automated stations along Interstate 80 from Table Rock to around Wamsutter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of over 40 mph were reported at both Continental Divide and Tipton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept south through northern Johnson County shortly after 1700MST. The passage of the front brought high winds from the northwest to several locations along Interstate 90 during an approximate three-hour period. Peak wind gusts topped out between 60 and 71 mph. The highest gust of 71 mph was recorded along the interstate north of Buffalo at 1706MST. Other measured gusts included 66 mph at the Buffalo Airport ASOS and 62 mph at an automated site along the interstate east of Buffalo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Piney Creek WYDOT sensor hit 71 mph, while the Buffalo Airport ASOS reported a wind gusts to 66 mph.  WYDOT Indian Creek sensor was sustained at 40mph+ until 2020MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of early spring winter storms hit portions of western and central Wyoming beginning on April 30. The Lander Foothills were once again the hardest hit area with some locations receiving over a foot of additional snow. The conclusion of this episode is continued in May 2013 Storm Data.
A low pressure system over western South Dakota generated strong and gusty northwest winds to southeast Wyoming.  Gusts of 60 to 65 mph were observed over the southern Laramie Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The UPR sensor at Emkay measured wind gusts of 58 mph or higher, with a peak gust of 63 mph at 31/1155 MST.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL LARAMIE RANGE AND SOUTHWEST PLATTE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large pressure gradient with strong westerly winds aloft produced high winds with frequent gusts above 60 mph.  Peak wind gusts approached 80 mph over the southern Laramie Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at Bordeaux measured sustained winds of 40 mph or higher, with a peak gust of 59 mph at 30/0850 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous spring storm brought heavy snow and strong wind to much of central and southern Wyoming. Many areas recorded over a foot of snow. The highest snowfall amounts were recorded along the east slope of the Wind River Range and the Lander Foothills where amounts over 18 inches were common. A maximum snowfall total of 28.5 inches was recorded 3 miles southwest of Lander. Other hard hit areas included Casper and much of Natrona County where 12 to 18 inches of snow was observed; around Riverton where 6 to 12 inches was common; and near Thermopolis where up to 16 inches of snow was recorded. In addition, the storm brought damaging winds to southern Wyoming, particularly Sweetwater County. Wind gusts to 71 mph where recorded at the Rock Springs-Sweetwater County Airport. Wind damage was recorded in Green River where several trees where downed and a window was blown in at Western Wyoming Community College in Rock Springs. At Flaming Gorge Reservoir, a couple of docks were damaged at the Buckboard Marina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of heavy snow across the Wind River Basin. Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 inches in Shoshoni to 10 to 12 inches in Riverton and Hudson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a progression of shortwave troughs moving through Wyoming brought a third straight day of severe weather to western and central Wyoming. There were numerous reports of hail with the largest diameter around one inch. There were also multiple reports of strong winds including a 68 mph wind gust at Hiland in Natrona County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph at the North Big Horn County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a couple of weak disturbances moving through the area and an approaching upper level wind maxima provided the necessary fuel for strong to severe thunderstorms. A supercell was observed across Teton County while additional severe storms tracked across the eastern Big Horn Basin and Johnson County. There were numerous reports of hail over one inch in diameter with golf ball sized hail reported in Johnson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported half dollar sized hail approximately six miles northwest of Buffalo.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust around 70 mph resulted in about 8 trees either toppled or broken in the Story area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant low-level moisture coupled with an unstable atmosphere and an approaching jet streak produced several severe thunderstorms over Johnson County on the evening of the 26th. The hardest hit areas were just north of Kaycee and along Interstate 90 near the crossing of the Powder River. The largest stones fell near the Powder River Rest Area where 1.5 inch diameter hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch diameter hail was reported about 3 miles north-northeast of Kaycee. The hail broke out windows in an antique car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lived winter storm developed over the Great Basin on April 15 and tracked slowly across Colorado through April 17. Periods of snow broke out early in the morning of the 15th across the Lander Foothills and spread north and east through the day. Heavy snow fell across much of central Wyoming with the greatest accumulations extending east from the eastern slopes of the Wind River Range through the Lander Foothills and into Natrona County. Casper Mountain was the hardest hit with one report of 56 inches of snow and widespread reports of around 3 feet. Most of the Lander Foothills received 1 to 2 feet of snow. Around the lower elevations of Natrona County, including Casper, 1 foot or more of snow was reported in many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of 6 to 11 inches of snow in Riverton were received. 8 inches of snow was reported at Hudson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm rolled south off the Absaroka Mountains and across the Upper Wind River Valley. The storm produced hail in and around Dubois some of which reached the size of quarters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch diameter hail was reported by a trained spotter on the west side of Dubois.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lived winter storm developed over the Great Basin on April 15 and tracked slowly across Colorado through April 17. Periods of snow broke out early in the morning of the 15th across the Lander Foothills and spread north and east through the day. Heavy snow fell across much of central Wyoming with the greatest accumulations extending east from the eastern slopes of the Wind River Range through the Lander Foothills and into Natrona County. Casper Mountain was the hardest hit with one report of 56 inches of snow and widespread reports of around 3 feet. Most of the Lander Foothills received 1 to 2 feet of snow. Around the lower elevations of Natrona County, including Casper, 1 foot or more of snow was reported in many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the eastern slopes of the Wind River Mountains. 18 inches of snow was reported at the St. Lawrence Alt SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong west to southwest wind was prevalent across the Cody Foothills east of the Absaroka Range ahead of an approaching cold front. The driver was injured and the fertilizer and diesel fuel were spilled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a couple of weak disturbances moving through the area and an approaching upper level wind maxima provided the necessary fuel for strong to severe thunderstorms. There were numerous reports of hail over one inch in diameter with golf ball sized hail reported in Johnson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous storm spotters reported pea to quarter sized hail in Jackson.

Snow began April 30 and continued into May 1 in the Lander Foothills and western portions of the Green River Basin. Total snowfall topped-out around one foot in the Lander Foothills. A narrow band of snow developed over the western Green River Basin and produced around one foot of snow in Big Piney and Marbleton. Most of this snow fell in about a six hour period. The beginning of this event is contained in April 2013 Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lander airport recorded 13.1 inches of snow from the evening of April 30 through May 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent, moist, north-northwest flow aloft brought heavy snow to the northern Bighorn Mountains, especially above about 8500 feet. Two day snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 3 feet in the vicinity of Bald Mountain near US Highway 14A. The snow toppled trees and caused damage to trailers located at a lodge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 36 inches of snow at the High Country Lodge in the Big Horn Mountains. The Bald Mountain SNOTEL at 9380 feet elevation had an estimated 26 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient swept strong northwest winds across northern Johnson County on May 31. An automated sensor along Interstate 90 east of Buffalo had several wind gusts past 58 mph with a maximum gust of 65 mph. The ASOS at the Johnson County Airport in Buffalo topped-out at 61 mph and a nearby sensor reached 59 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at Indian Creek recorded several wind gusts past 58 mph with a maximum gust of 65 mph.

A line of thunderstorms developed over the southern Big Horn Basin and become severe as they progressed to the northeast. Hail was reported in multiple locations. The largest hail was quarter sized and was observed in Winchester. Several funnel clouds were reported in Washakie County with a rotating wall cloud reported north of Worland. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm along the Idaho-Wyoming border weakened as it moved east across Teton County. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty outflow wind from a collapsing rain shower blew down or broke over 100 trees at Colter Bay Campground and RV Park. Three or four of the falling trees struck and damaged vehicles at the park. One large branch, estimated to be 40 feet long and nearly one foot in diameter, struck a 30-year-old woman. The woman was life-flighted to a regional hospital with critical injuries.

The largest hail was quarter sized and was observed in Winchester. Several funnel clouds were reported in Washakie County with a rotating wall cloud reported north of Worland. Heavy rain in and on the steep slopes just east of Medicine Lodge State Archaeological Site resulted in flash flooding in that park. One main road and six campsites were damaged as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported several funnel clouds as well as a rotating wall cloud north of Worland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed before noon in a favorable environment over north-central Wyoming. The storms eventually developed farther to the east on either side of the Bighorn Mountains. One storm spawned a weak tornado over open country in eastern Park County. Another storm eventually produced large hail near Ten Sleep, while smaller hail fell in southern Johnson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to one inch in diameter fell at a ranch 9 miles northwest of Ten Sleep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous spring storm brought heavy snow and strong wind to much of central and southern Wyoming. Many areas recorded over a foot of snow. The highest snowfall amounts were recorded along the east slope of the Wind River Range and the Lander Foothills where amounts over 18 inches were common. A maximum snowfall total of 28.5 inches was recorded 3 miles southwest of Lander. Other hard hit areas included Casper and much of Natrona County where 12 to 18 inches of snow was observed; around Riverton where 6 to 12 inches was common; and near Thermopolis where up to 16 inches of snow was recorded. In addition, the storm brought damaging winds to southern Wyoming, particularly Sweetwater County. Wind gusts to 71 mph where recorded at the Rock Springs-Sweetwater County Airport. Wind damage was recorded in Green River where several trees where downed and a window was blown in at Western Wyoming Community College in Rock Springs. At Flaming Gorge Reservoir, a couple of docks were damaged at the Buckboard Marina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of heavy snow throughout Natrona County. The city of Casper received anywhere from 6 to 14 inches, 12 inches was reported at Evansville, 10 inches at Mills and 9 inches at Bar Nunn.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms brought flash flooding to the Medicine Lodge State Archaeological Site. Debris covered the main road leading into the park in several spots, occasionally to a depth of one to two feet. At least six campsites in the park were damaged when normally dry arroyos to the east of the park swept water and debris across them before emptying into Medicine Lodge Creek. One camper was damaged as mud and rock flowed against its sides. Rainfall estimates ranged from 0.50 inches to 1.25 inches in about 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived winter storm moved across western and central Wyoming and brought heavy snow to many areas, especially the western mountains. The highest accumulations were in the Salt River and Wyoming ranges where up to 30 inches fell at the Blind Bull SNOTEL. Accumulations of 1 to 2 feet were common across the higher elevations of Yellowstone National Park, and the Teton and Wind River ranges. In the lower elevations, the snow mixed with rain at times, limiting accumulation. Maximum accumulations in the lower elevations topped out around 1 foot in Bondurant and at 7 inches in Green River. The storm was accompanied by gusty northeast wind and arctic temperatures. The two combined to create wind chill readings well below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL stations at Beartooth Lake and Evening Star estimated 19 inches of snow. 18 inches of new snow was estimated at the Blackwater SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropping south from Montana brought strong low-level northwest flow to northern Johnson County. This favorable wind direction allowed for sustained winds of over 40 mph for several hours at two mesonet sensors alongside Interstate 90. The strongest winds occurred between about 1100 and 1200MST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensors at Indian Creek Road and Piney Creek recorded sustained winds greater than 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance and strong northwest flow aloft brought heavy snow to portions of the Bighorn Range as well as the eastern Big Horn Basin and Star Valley. Snowfall amounts as high as 34 inches were recorded in the higher elevations of the northwest Bighorn Range. Amounts in the eastern Big Horn Basin were as high as 6 inches with up to 8 inches in the Star Valley. In addition, strong winds occurred across the Upper Wind River Basin with wind gusts as high as 58 mph at Dubois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph wind gust was recorded at Dubois.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front and upper level storm system moved across the northern Plains; bringing snow, gusty winds, and very cold air to the region. The heaviest snow fell across northern Campbell and Crook Counties, where snowfall amounts were in the four to eight inch range. Gusty winds produced considerable blowing and drifting snow, with greatly reduced visibilities at times.

A tight pressure and thermal gradient developed over the Absaroka Range and strong gap flow resulted in high winds. The winds were strongest in the wind prone areas of the Cody Foothills, especially around Clark. High winds persisted over portions of three days with repeated wind gusts above 60 mph at a sensor along State Highway 120 south of Clark. This sensor measured a peak gust of 74 mph at 0800MST December 15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were many wind gusts past 58 mph near Clark. The maximum gusts was 74 mph 8 miles south-southwest of Clark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasons first arctic outbreak and combined winter storm moved into the Billings Forecast Area during the first week of December. Mild temperatures ended the month of November and continued through the morning of the 2nd. A strong disturbance moved across the area during the afternoon of the 2nd. This disturbance combined with temperatures around 50 degrees for most of the day, resulted in a period of light to moderate rain for a few hours. An arctic front moved into the area by late in the afternoon turning the rain to snow. Temperatures quickly dropped into the teens and single digits during the overnight hours. The initial wet snow that fell onto already wet pavement from the rain quickly froze which resulted in very icy road conditions. The snow, which was heavy at times, continued through the 3rd before ending on the 4th. However, bitterly cold temperatures continued through the 8th with daytime highs only in the single digits above and below zero with overnight lows in the teens below to 30s below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 12 inches along with blowing snow was reported. Interstate 90 was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The moist Pacific storm system that moved into Utah on December 19 also impacted far southwest Wyoming. There was no freezing rain reported in Wyoming, but periods of heavy snowfall did impact the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Evanston received 8.5 inches of new snow, while Mountain View reported a storm total of 4 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent early season winter storm moved into Wyoming early on Thursday, October 3, and continued through much of Friday, October 4. Snow began in the higher elevations of western Wyoming early Thursday morning. Rain across the lower elevations changed to snow during the afternoon and evening hours of Thursday as colder air moved across Wyoming from west to east. With impressive upper level dynamics and ample moisture, snowfall rates approached two inches an hour at some locations.||The wet, heavy snow fell on trees that still had full foliage and brought many limbs and trees down onto streets and power lines. Natrona County was hardest hit with 14,000 customers without power at the peak of the storm. Several warming shelters were established Friday along the I-25 corridor to help those without heat. The heavy snow also brought many road closures to central Wyoming. In Casper, snowfall of 16.2 inches was the tenth highest storm total since records began in 1937 and held a liquid water content of 2.14 inches. The highest snowfall amounts fell in the higher elevations with over two feet of snow recorded in the higher elevations of the Wind River, Bighorn, and Absaroka ranges, as well as Casper Mountain. The highest amount was at the Reno Hill SNOTEL where 34 inches of snow was recorded. Many lower elevation locations east of the Continental Divide reported 6 to 12 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 10 to 12 inches of snow in Wamsutter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused the Alum Fire in a remote area south of Hayden Valley in Yellowstone National Park. The fire was first seen on Wednesday morning, August 14, and showed little growth over the following two days. However, a hot, dry airmass coupled with wind speeds of 10 to 20 mph whipped the flames on Saturday afternoon, August 17. The fire erupted from a Saturday morning size of 3 acres to an estimated 3,000 acres by the end of the day. The fire crowned in mature lodgepole pine and advanced six miles to the east-northeast in just a few hours. The hot and dry conditions continued into Sunday and the fire expanded by another 1,000 acres.||By Tuesday, August 20, the fire was close enough to threaten several tourist attractions, including Mud Volcano and LeHardy Rapids, and the Grand Loop Road. The proximity of the fire caused the closure late Tuesday afternoon of a seven and a half mile section of the road from near Mud Volcano to Hayden Valley. At one point the fire came within 200 yards of the road. The fire was estimated to be about 7,000 acres in size on August 21, when the road was re-opened to traffic. The fire topped-out around 7,100 acres in size by August 24, before beneficial thunderstorm rains dampened fire activity over the last week of August.

With impressive upper level dynamics and ample moisture, snowfall rates approached two inches an hour at some locations.||The wet, heavy snow fell on trees that still had full foliage and brought many limbs and trees down onto streets and power lines. Natrona County was hardest hit with 14,000 customers without power at the peak of the storm. Several warming shelters were established Friday along the I-25 corridor to help those without heat. The heavy snow also brought many road closures to central Wyoming. In Casper, snowfall of 16.2 inches was the tenth highest storm total since records began in 1937 and held a liquid water content of 2.14 inches. The highest snowfall amounts fell in the higher elevations with over two feet of snow recorded in the higher elevations of the Wind River, Bighorn, and Absaroka ranges, as well as Casper Mountain. The highest amount was at the Reno Hill SNOTEL where 34 inches of snow was recorded. Many lower elevation locations east of the Continental Divide reported 6 to 12 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy snow fell at SNOTEL stations in the western Bighorn Mountains. Some of these included 27 inches at Middle Powder, 18 inches at Shell Creek and 17 inches at Powder River Pass.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Highway 85 south of Hawk Springs was ice-covered from freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system in the Great Basin brought moisture and colder air to western Wyoming. Many lower elevation locations received three to six inches of snow. The heaviest low elevation snow fell in Jackson Hole, especially in and around the town of Jackson. Snowfall totals of six to nine inches were common. One report from a private meteorologist indicated about five inches fell in two hours on the evening of Monday, October 28. He reported another 4 to 5 inches falling by sunrise Tuesday. Southerly flow aloft, the position of an upper level jet stream, and local terrain effects helped to generate the higher totals at Jackson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported from 6 to 9 inches across the Jackson Hole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong early Fall storm system moved into the Northern Rockies bringing significant snow to portions of the Billings Forecast Area. Heavy snow fell mainly along and south of a Livingston-Billings-Miles City-Baker Montana line. The heaviest snowfall occurred along the eastern foothills of the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains where up to two feet of snow fell. The main impacts from this storm were significant tree damage along with power outages as deciduous trees were still holding their leaves and northeast winds gusting over 20 mph added additional stress to trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of tree damage and power outages due to the heavy, wet snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A RAWS sensor near Muddy Gap measured wind gusts of 58 mph or higher, with a peak gust of 71 mph at 01/1550 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dropped very heavy rain over portions of eastern Lincoln County during the late afternoon hours of September 12. Rainfall estimated at around one inch fell in a little less than one hour. Flash flooding caused damage to 25 homes, most of which were in the town of Labarge, and along about a 3.6 mile stretch of US Highway 189 south of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms brought flooding and mudslides in, and to the south of, LaBarge. The flooding damaged 20 homes located mainly in the southwest portion of the town. Much of the flooding was confined to basements of homes and at least one church. High water scoured the channel of Birch Creek at the north end of town and exposed water and sewer pipes.||To the south of town, approximately four separate large channels along with many smaller rivulets flooded US Highway 189 between mileposts 77 and 81. The Wyoming Highway Patrol reported a mudslide near milepost 78 around Muddy Creek at 1651MST. Farther north near Names Hill another small canyon deposited mud and rock across the highway and onto private land. Five homes were damaged in the county, one of which was declared a total loss. Multiple sections of fence line were either washed away or completely buried by debris. Mud and rock plugged culverts along the highway and also buried culvert inlets. This set the stage for additional flooding across US Highway 189 over the course of the next several days.

Across central Wyoming, wind gusts of around 60 mph were common from the Granite Mountains to Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind gusts of over 75 mph were recorded near Clark. The strongest recorded gust was 97 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low brought unseasonably moist air to southwest Wyoming. The result was heavy thunderstorm rains across eastern Lincoln County where rainfall earlier in the week set the stage for flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Culverts that were plugged and buried by mud and rock from a flash flood on September 12, were unable to handle another deluge of water on the afternoon of Saturday, September 14. The heavy rain caused mudslides that covered portions of US Highway 189 south of LaBarge between mileposts 80 and 82. The road was closed at 1700MST Saturday to enable the Wyoming Department of Transportation to clean-up the debris. The road was opened the morning of September 15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving across western and central Wyoming spawned several severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Hail up to 1.25 inches in diameter fell near Daniel with numerous other reports of hail from pea to nickel size. Wind gusts of 60 mph were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 8 miles north of Daniel reported half-dollar sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong west to southwest flow aloft generated strong winds in and near the Absaroka Range and in central Wyoming. Across central Wyoming, wind gusts of around 60 mph were common from the Granite Mountains to Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weather sensor on Chief Joseph Pass recorded several wind gusts over 75 mph, including a maximum gust of 94 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low and favorable jet dynamics combined to produce an early season snowstorm across many areas east of the Continental Divide. The heaviest accumulations of snow fell across portions of Fremont County from Riverton through Lander into the Wind River Range. Accumulations of 6 to 8 inches were common across the lower elevations to over a foot in the Wind River Range. The heavy, wet snow fell on trees that still had full foliage. As a result there was widespread tree damage across much of central Fremont County resulting in blocked roads and numerous power outages. At the peak of the storm more than 7000 customers had lost power due to limbs landing on power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed over to snow on the evening of September 26 and continued through the following morning. Heavy, wet snow accumulations generally ranged from 4 to 7 inches across the area. With trees still having full leaf coverage, there was widespread tree damage throughout the region with downed limbs blocking streets and causing many power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong west to southwest flow aloft generated strong winds in and near the Absaroka Range and in central Wyoming. Across central Wyoming, wind gusts of around 60 mph were common from the Granite Mountains to Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust to 60 mph was recorded at Twenty Mile Hill. The Casper Airport ASOS clocked a gust of 59 mph shortly after 1200MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low over southwest Wyoming provided instability and spin to the atmospshere. A thunderstorm spawned a brief tornado off of Blue Rim Road in Sweetwater County, near the intersection of State Routes 372 and 28. The tornado was observed up-close and from two different distant vantage points, but remained over open country and caused no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public posted a picture of a tornado off of Blue Rim Road, approximately 18 miles southwest of Eden. Other public reports were received of seeing the tornado from near Seedskadee and Eden. The tornado remained over open country and caused no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms generated several reports of large hail, heavy rain and gusty winds across portions of southeast Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball to tennis ball size hail was reported near Federal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and early evening thunderstorms produced large hail, strong winds and very heavy rainfall over much of southeast Wyoming.  Flash flooding was observed north of Cheyenne.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball size hail was reported east of Hawk Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed just south of Echeta and tracked slowly northward across northwestern Campbell County. The storm produced large hail at times from the Echeta area to the Montana border.
A late afternoon thunderstorm produced large hail in west central Albany County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to quarter size hail was reported west of Bosler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry lower atmosphere set the stage for gusty outflow wind from weak convection across southwest Wyoming. A dissipating storm near Rock Springs produced a peak wind gust of 75 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outflow from a dissipating thunderstorm produced strong and erratic outflow wind at the Rock Springs-Sweetwater County Airport. A peak gust of 75 mph was clocked at 1430MST from the southwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon thunderstorms over Laramie County produced numerous reports of large hail, along with heavy rain and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported in Cheyenne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms erupted over the eastern Wind River Mountains during the morning hours and moved east-northeast across Fremont and Natrona counties. A favorable wind profile helped the storms become severe and produce hail up to the size of baseballs at Powder River. There were numerous reports of quarter to golf ball sized hail in a swath from Castle Gardens to around Natrona. As the storm approached Casper at least three funnel clouds were observed, one of which briefly touched down northeast of Evansville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed about 20 miles northwest of Casper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second day of severe weather occurred across central Wyoming on August 9. An unstable and moist atmosphere helped to generate very heavy rain, strong wind gusts, and hail up to nickel size. One strong storm produced heavy rain and strong outflow wind as it tracked east-southeast across northern Natrona County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell in the Buffalo and Cottonwood creek drainages in northwest Natrona County along the extreme southeast slope of the Bighorn Mountains. A nearly stationary thunderstorm dropped rainfall estimated at over one to two inches in one hour. Run-off quickly reached Buffalo Creek along the Buffalo Creek Road near a rural ranch. The creek swelled to be many times its normal size and flooded the road near the ranch. Debris was left on the roadway in the wake of the flood and a five-mile stretch of the road needed repair.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced strong wind gusts and heavy rain over northern Campbell County into Crook County. Three to five inches of rain fell in a few hours, causing minor flooding along rural roads in the Recluse area of northern Campbell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail drifts and water covered portions of Sam Adam Road and Broyles Road when drainage culverts were quickly overwhelmed from heavy rain. Three to four inches of water covered US Highway 14-16 near Spotted Horse. Nearly-dry stock ponds quickly filled with the runoff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass and an upper level disturbance brought a third straight day of thunderstorms to western and central Wyoming. The strongest thunderstorm formed over the Absaroka Range and become severe as it moved east across the Big Horn Basin and into Johnson County. There were numerous reports of large hail in a swath from near Barnum to Midwest with the largest stones being ping pong ball sized in Midwest. The storm also brought wind gusts up to 60 mph. In addition, a wind gust from a collapsing thunderstorm in Sublette County produced a 59 mph wind gust at the Big Piney Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported near Sunshine Reservoir.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance and associated cooling aloft helped to generate showers and thunderstorms over eastern Idaho during the afternoon. A couple of these storms became severe over the far western valleys of Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter at Star Valley Ranch reported hail up to the size of quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed in the Gillette area and remained nearly stationary for a time, then tracked very slowly northward. The storm produced hail up half dollar size and heavy rain over northern Campbell County.

The combination of a couple of weak disturbances moving through the area and an approaching upper level wind maxima provided the necessary fuel for strong to severe thunderstorms. A supercell was observed across Teton County while additional severe storms tracked across the eastern Big Horn Basin and Johnson County. There were numerous reports of hail over one inch in diameter with golf ball sized hail reported in Johnson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer near Emblem had penny sized hail at their residence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed west of Gillette and tracked northeastward across northern Campbell and Crook Counties. The storm produced quarter size hail north of Gillette. It strengthened over Crook County and produced tennis ball size hail around Devils Tower, which damaged automobiles.
Hail to half dollar size fell near Echeta.

A cluster of thunderstorms produced gusty winds and heavy rain across southern Weston County. Two to three inches of rain in less than an hour caused flash flooding across portions of eastern Weston County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist northwest flow aloft combined with a series of upper level disturbances to produce a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snowfall.  Heaviest snowfall amounts were estimated between 3 and 5 feet over the higher peaks of the Snowy and Sierra Madre ranges.  The upper North Platte River basin received four to eight inches of snow.  West to northwest winds gusting to 60 mph produced near zero visibilities in blowing and drifting on open mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Brooklyn Lake SNOTEL site (elevation 10240 ft) estimated 30 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sand Lake SNOTEL site (elevation 10050 ft) estimated ten inches of snow.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL LARAMIE RANGE AND SOUTHWEST PLATTE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient along the Laramie Range generated a few brief periods of strong west to southwest surface winds near Bordeaux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at Bordeaux measured sustained winds of 40 mph or higher.
